title: Chip Stocks Tumble Into a Bear Market as China's Kimi K3 Sparks a Fresh 'DeepSeek Moment'
content: Semiconductor shares slumped on Friday after Chinese start-up Moonshot unveiled Kimi K3, a 2.8-trillion-parameter open-weight model that reportedly rivals the best US systems, reviving fears that cheaper Chinese AI could undercut the West's data-centre spending boom. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell as much as 5.7% on the day and has now dropped more than 20% from its late-June record, tipping the benchmark into a bear market and dragging the Nasdaq down 1.5%.
summary: A new Chinese AI model rattled the AI-hardware trade, pushing the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index more than 20% below its June peak and into a bear market.
context:
• Chip valuations had priced in near-perfect, uninterrupted AI demand after a blistering 2026 rally, leaving little cushion for disappointment in a historically cyclical industry.

• Traders drew immediate parallels with early-2025's 'DeepSeek moment', when a low-cost Chinese model briefly wiped hundreds of billions from AI names.

• Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing had already unsettled the sector this week by guiding to higher-than-expected capital spending, partly on rising tool prices.
happened:
• Moonshot's Kimi K3, billed as the largest open-weight model yet at 2.8 trillion parameters, was reported to match or beat several leading US models on benchmarks, trailing only the top US frontier systems.

• The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell as much as 5.7% and closed the week more than 20% below its late-June high, with global chip stocks shedding roughly $3.3tn in value since 22 June.

• Applied Materials, Lam Research, Intel, KLA and Arm each fell about 4%, Nvidia and Micron dropped more than 2%, and the Nasdaq lost 1.5% on Friday to end the week down 2.9%.
implications:
• A sustained rethink of AI capital expenditure would hit the entire chip supply chain, from foundries and memory makers to equipment vendors and power suppliers.

• Cheaper, capable open-weight models could compress the pricing premium on cutting-edge accelerators and pressure hyperscaler margins.

• Expect heightened volatility around forthcoming Big Tech capex guidance, which the market will now scrutinise for any sign of restraint.
importance:
• Semiconductors have been the single biggest driver of the 2026 equity rally, so a bear market in the group is a systemic risk to index returns.

• The episode tests the core bull thesis that AI demand is structural rather than cyclical.

• A credible low-cost Chinese challenger reshapes the competitive map for US chip and software leaders alike.

title: Apple Briefly Retakes Most-Valuable Crown From Nvidia as AI Rotation Bites
content: Apple momentarily overtook Nvidia as the world's most valuable company on Friday, its market capitalisation touching about $4.88tn against Nvidia's roughly $4.86tn after the AI bellwether slid around 3.5% in the chip sell-off. The positions later flipped back, with Nvidia edging ahead by the close, but the tussle underscored a broadening rotation out of heavy AI spenders and towards Apple's lighter capital-expenditure model.
summary: Apple briefly leapfrogged Nvidia to become the world's largest company as investors rotated away from capital-intensive AI names.
context:
• Nvidia had held the title of world's most valuable company since June 2025, when it overtook Microsoft on surging AI-accelerator demand.

• Apple has climbed almost 23% in 2026, outpacing the market as investors reward its services growth and comparatively modest AI outlays.

• The move fits a wider late-cycle rotation as markets question the durability of the AI capital-spending boom.
happened:
• Apple's market value briefly reached about $4.88tn versus Nvidia's roughly $4.86tn during Friday's session before the ranking reversed by the close.

• Nvidia fell around 3.5% as the Chinese-AI scare and profit-taking hit the semiconductor complex.

• The swing came on a broadly negative day for megacap tech, with the S&P 500 down 1% and the Nasdaq off 1.5%.
implications:
• Leadership rotating from chipmakers to cash-generative, lower-capex megacaps could reshape index composition and factor performance.

• Continued scrutiny of AI returns on investment may keep the most capital-hungry names under pressure.

• A crowded, top-heavy market means small shifts among the largest stocks carry outsized index effects.
importance:
• The identity of the world's largest company is a barometer for which narrative — AI infrastructure or platform profitability — the market is rewarding.

• Concentration risk is acute, with a handful of names driving the bulk of index returns.

• The rotation offers a tell on how investors are repositioning for a possible AI-spending slowdown.

title: Oil Surges More Than 10% on the Week as US-Iran Clashes Threaten Hormuz
content: Crude climbed to one-month highs on Friday, with Brent trading above $80 and around $86 a barrel, capping a weekly gain of more than 10% as renewed fighting between US and Iranian forces revived fears of a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. The rally has reignited inflation worries just as cooling June price data had begun to soothe markets, lifting bets on a Federal Reserve rate rise later this year.
summary: Brent rose above $80 and towards $86 a barrel, up more than 10% on the week, as US-Iran hostilities threatened Gulf supply.
context:
• The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil, leaving prices acutely sensitive to any threat to Gulf shipping.

• A fragile US-Iran ceasefire had begun to reopen the waterway before this week's escalation reversed much of that relief.

• The spike lands just after a soft June inflation print, complicating the disinflation narrative that markets had started to embrace.
happened:
• Brent traded above $80 and near $86 a barrel on Friday, returning to one-month highs and leaving crude up more than 10% over the week.

• Renewed US-Iran clashes and fears of a Hormuz blockade drove the advance, adding a fresh geopolitical risk premium.

• The oil shock pushed some gauges of September Fed rate-hike odds sharply higher, reviving the inflation debate.
implications:
• A sustained crude spike would feed straight into headline inflation and stiffen central banks' resolve to keep policy tight.

• Any move to disrupt Hormuz traffic in earnest would risk a far larger supply shock than currently priced.

• Diplomacy remains the swing factor, leaving prices hostage to headlines from the Gulf.
importance:
• Oil is the principal channel through which Middle East geopolitics transmits into global inflation and rate expectations.

• A renewed energy shock directly complicates the Fed's policy calculus into the autumn.

• Crude swings ripple across equity sectors, credit spreads and emerging-market currencies.

title: European Shares End Flat as US Tech Rout Offsets a Broadening Rally
content: The pan-European STOXX 600 slipped 0.34% to 641.53 on Friday but held roughly flat for the week, as a global sell-off in technology shares offset a rally that has been broadening into defensives. Chip-exposed names led the declines, with ASML down 3.9%, Infineon off 4.8% and STMicroelectronics falling 5.8%, while utilities and consumer staples such as Vodafone and National Grid provided ballast ahead of next week's European Central Bank meeting.
summary: The STOXX 600 finished roughly flat on the week as a US-led tech rout was cushioned by gains in defensive sectors before the ECB meeting.
context:
• A softer-than-expected June inflation reading has cooled the case for further ECB tightening, even as oil's rebound clouds the outlook.

• Banks including UBS and Deutsche Bank lifted their STOXX 600 targets this week, citing a rally that is spreading beyond technology.

• Europe's chipmakers are heavily tied to the same AI-spending narrative now under pressure in the United States.
happened:
• The STOXX 600 eased 0.34% to 641.53 on Friday; the Euro Stoxx 50 closed up 0.25% at 6,281, the FTSE 100 rose 0.54% to 10,572, the DAX fell 0.53% to 24,866 and the CAC 40 was little changed at 8,378.

• Semiconductor names ASML, Infineon and STMicroelectronics were among the biggest fallers, tracking the US chip slump.

• Defensives outperformed, with Vodafone up 3.0% and National Grid up 2.2%, as investors sought shelter.
implications:
• Next week's ECB decision and guidance will set the near-term tone for European rates and equities.

• A rotation into utilities and staples signals more cautious positioning even as headline indices hold up.

• Renewed energy-price pressure could revive core-inflation worries and delay any further easing.
importance:
• European equities are testing whether a broadening rally can withstand a US-led tech drawdown.

• The ECB meeting is a key catalyst for euro-zone bonds, the single currency and rate-sensitive sectors.

• Europe's chip exposure links its benchmarks directly to the global AI-hardware debate.

title: Treasury Yields Ease as Data Signals the Economy Is Weathering the Iran Shock
content: US Treasury yields drifted lower on Friday, with the 10-year note at about 4.53% and the two-year near 4.12%, as a run of data suggested the economy is absorbing the inflationary jolt from the Iran conflict without buckling. The move capped a choppy week in rates, with markets weighing cooling June inflation against an oil-driven pickup in price risks and the Federal Reserve's shift to pure data dependence.
summary: The 10-year Treasury yield eased to around 4.53% as resilient data reassured markets the economy is withstanding the oil-price shock.
context:
• Under Chair Kevin Warsh the Fed has held its policy rate at 3.50%-3.75% and dropped traditional forward guidance in favour of pure data dependence.

• A soft June inflation print pulled the annual rate down to 3.5%, tempering near-term tightening bets.

• The rebound in oil prices has reintroduced upside inflation risk, keeping the rates outlook finely balanced.
happened:
• The 10-year yield slipped to about 4.53% and the two-year to roughly 4.12% on Friday as data pointed to a resilient economy.

• The move reversed part of an earlier back-up in yields tied to the crude spike and firmer hike expectations.

• Markets have largely ruled out a July move while remaining split on the odds of a September hike.
implications:
• With guidance stripped away, every inflation and labour print now carries outsized weight for the rates path.

• A durable oil-price spike could reignite the sell-off in bonds and push yields back up.

• The tug-of-war between cooling core inflation and energy-led risk will define positioning into the late-July Fed meeting.
importance:
• Treasury yields are the anchor for global borrowing costs and the discount rate on risk assets.

• The data-dependence regime raises the market impact of each macro release.

• Rate moves of this kind feed directly into equities, credit and the dollar.

title: Dollar Set for Weekly Loss as Softer Inflation Pares Fed Hike Bets
content: The dollar steadied around 100.7 on the dollar index on Friday but was on course for a weekly decline, after cooler-than-expected US inflation prompted traders to trim expectations for near-term Federal Reserve rate rises. The euro held near $1.14 and the yen stayed soft, leaving the greenback caught between fading tightening bets and bouts of safe-haven demand from the Middle East conflict.
summary: The dollar index steadied near 100.7 but headed for a weekly loss as softer inflation reduced near-term Fed hike expectations.
context:
• June inflation cooled more than forecast, undercutting the case for imminent Fed tightening and weighing on the dollar.

• The Fed has held rates at 3.50%-3.75% and now leans purely on incoming data, amplifying the currency's sensitivity to each print.

• Escalating US-Iran tensions have periodically revived haven demand, partly offsetting the softer-rates drag.
happened:
• The dollar index hovered around 100.7 on Friday but remained on track for a weekly fall as hike bets were pared.

• The euro traded near $1.14, holding recent ranges, while the yen stayed weak.

• The currency's path reflected a tug-of-war between fading tightening expectations and intermittent geopolitical haven flows.
implications:
• A softer dollar eases global financial conditions and offers relief to emerging-market borrowers and commodity importers.

• Any fresh Middle East escalation could quickly restore haven demand and reverse the move.

• With rate expectations finely poised, each data release now carries heightened currency risk.
importance:
• The dollar is the market's cleanest cross-asset read on shifting Fed expectations and global risk appetite.

• Currency swings ripple through dollar-priced commodities and cross-border capital flows.

• The balance between haven demand and rate bets frames positioning into the late-July Fed meeting.

title: Gold Slides Below $4,000, Heads for Weekly Loss as Oil Keeps Rate Fears Alive
content: Gold held below $4,000 an ounce on Friday and was on track to lose more than 3% for the week, its weakest levels since late 2025, as an oil-driven revival in inflation worries kept expectations of higher-for-longer interest rates in play. Spot bullion traded near $3,986 and August futures around $3,980, with the surging crude price undercutting the appeal of the non-yielding metal.
summary: Gold slipped below $4,000 an ounce and was set for a weekly loss of more than 3% as oil-fuelled rate fears sapped demand.
context:
• Gold pays no yield, so firmer expectations for interest rates typically weigh on its appeal versus cash and bonds.

• The Middle East oil spike has revived inflation concerns that argue for tighter policy, pressuring bullion.

• The metal is retreating from lofty 2026 levels reached during earlier bouts of geopolitical stress.
happened:
• Spot gold traded near $3,986 an ounce and August futures around $3,980 on Friday, holding below the $4,000 mark.

• The metal was on course for a weekly drop of more than 3%, its softest since late 2025.

• Rising oil prices and firmer rate-hike bets combined to sap safe-haven demand for bullion.
implications:
• Continued strength in oil and rate expectations could keep gold on the back foot despite geopolitical risk.

• A sharper equity or geopolitical shock could nonetheless revive haven buying and stem the slide.

• The metal's next move hinges heavily on the interplay between inflation data and Fed guidance.
importance:
• Gold is a key barometer of real-rate expectations and risk sentiment across markets.

• A slide despite live geopolitical risk highlights how much rate expectations now dominate the metal.

• Bullion moves influence miners, central-bank reserve flows and broader haven positioning.

title: Bitcoin and Ether Slip as Iran Escalation Sours Risk Appetite
content: Cryptocurrencies eased on Friday as escalating US-Iran hostilities dented risk appetite, with bitcoin opening around $63,800, down 1.4% on the day, and ether near $1,863, off 2.8%. The pullback unwound part of a mid-week surge that had lifted bitcoin above $64,900 after a softer-than-expected US inflation report.
summary: Bitcoin slipped to around $63,800 and ether to about $1,863 as Middle East tensions weighed on risk sentiment.
context:
• Digital assets have increasingly traded as high-beta risk plays, tracking equities and shifts in Fed expectations.

• A soft June inflation print earlier in the week had powered a rally, with bitcoin climbing above $64,900.

• The renewed Iran conflict has since revived a broader risk-off tone across markets.
happened:
• Bitcoin opened near $63,800 on Friday, down 1.4% from Thursday, while ether fell 2.8% to around $1,863.

• The declines pared gains booked mid-week after cooler inflation data buoyed risk assets.

• The retreat mirrored weakness in equities as geopolitical tensions escalated.
implications:
• Crypto's tight correlation with equities leaves it exposed to any further risk-off move driven by the Gulf conflict.

• A durable easing in inflation and rate expectations could restore the mid-week bid.

• Sensitivity to macro data underscores how far digital assets now trade on the same signals as stocks.
importance:
• Bitcoin and ether are closely watched gauges of speculative risk appetite.

• Their reaction to macro prints highlights crypto's integration into mainstream cross-asset flows.

• Sharp swings carry read-through for crypto-linked equities and exchange-traded products.

title: US Inflation Cools Sharply to 3.5% in June as Energy Prices Slump
content: US consumer prices fell 0.4% in June, the biggest monthly drop since April 2020, pulling the annual inflation rate down to 3.5% from 4.2% in May and well below the 3.8% economists had expected. Core inflation, which strips out food and energy, was flat on the month for a 2.6% annual pace, cementing expectations that the Federal Reserve will hold rates steady at its next meeting even as a September move stays on the table.
summary: June CPI fell 0.4% on the month, cutting annual inflation to 3.5% and reinforcing bets the Fed will hold rates in July.
context:
• The Fed has kept its policy rate at 3.50%-3.75% and now relies on incoming data after dropping formal forward guidance.

• Inflation had proved sticky earlier in the year, with the May reading at 4.2%, keeping tightening risk alive.

• Energy prices had been a key swing factor, and June's drop reflected a sharp fall in petrol costs.
happened:
• Headline CPI fell 0.4% month-on-month and eased to 3.5% year-on-year, versus consensus for a 0.2% decline and a 3.8% annual rate.

• The energy index slumped 5.7% on the month, its largest drop since April 2020, while core CPI was flat for a 2.6% annual pace.

• Markets read the data as raising the odds of a July hold, with CME FedWatch showing roughly an 86% chance of no change, and equities and bitcoin rallied in response.
implications:
• A cooler core reading gives the Fed room to stay patient, though officials are wary of the oil-driven upside risk.

• The subsequent rebound in crude could partly reverse June's energy-led disinflation in coming prints.

• Continued softening would strengthen the case against further tightening later in the year.
importance:
• Inflation data is the single most important input for a Fed that has stripped out guidance in favour of the numbers.

• The print reset rate expectations across bonds, the dollar and risk assets in a single session.

• The tension between cooling core prices and rising energy costs will shape the policy debate into the autumn.

title: Wall Street Banks Post Record Q2 Profits as Trading and IPO Fees Surge
content: The five largest US banks all beat estimates for the second quarter, led by JPMorgan Chase's record $21.2bn profit — the highest ever for a US bank — and Goldman Sachs' best quarter in its history. Booming trading revenue and a bonanza of investment-banking fees, including from June's record-breaking SpaceX flotation, drove the results and underscored the health of the largest lenders even as markets turned volatile.
summary: JPMorgan posted a record $21.2bn quarterly profit and Goldman its best-ever quarter as US banks smashed Q2 estimates.
context:
• A revived capital-markets cycle, capped by SpaceX's roughly $86bn June listing at a $1.77tn valuation, has fuelled a surge in investment-banking fees.

• Elevated interest rates and busy markets have lifted both net interest income and trading revenue across Wall Street.

• Bank results are a widely watched read on the health of consumers, corporates and broader financial conditions.
happened:
• JPMorgan reported a record $21.2bn profit, up 41% year-on-year, with EPS of $6.14 versus $5.85 expected and revenue of $58.02bn.

• Goldman Sachs posted its best-ever quarter, with EPS of $20.98 — nearly double a year earlier — and revenue up 39% to $20.34bn.

• All five major lenders, including Bank of America, Citigroup and Wells Fargo, beat forecasts, buoyed by trading and IPO fees.
implications:
• Robust bank earnings point to resilient markets and dealmaking, supportive of the broader financial sector.

• A reliance on trading and IPO windfalls raises questions about how repeatable the record results are if markets cool.

• Strong balance sheets give the largest lenders room to return capital and absorb any downturn.
importance:
• The big banks kick off earnings season and set the tone for financials and the wider market.

• Record trading and banking fees confirm a reopened capital-markets cycle after years of drought.

• Bank profitability is a bellwether for credit conditions and the health of the real economy.
